stories.headline: US extends Iran air campaign after deaths of American troops

sources.body:
After the US military confirmed that a service member had been killed in Iraq – the third in recent days – Donald Trump said the new attacks were ‘in honour’ of the dead

Middle East crisis live: follow latest updates

The US launched a ninth night of strikes targeting Iran early Monday, after announcing the death of another American service member, as the price of Brent crude oil climbed above $90 a barrel, the highest level since June.

The US military said the service member was killed on Saturday in northern Iraq, where the US has military bases, during a controlled detonation of what was called unexploded ordnance on a downed Iranian attack drone.

“We hit them very hard again tonight,” Donald Trump said as he returned to Washington after the World Cup final, adding “we did that in honour of the, probably three, it’s probably three great patriots.”

It was the third US death in recent days, after two military service personnel were killed in an Iranian attack on a Jordanian airbase on Friday, with a third missing in action. Since the war began, 17 US service members have been killed.

As the strikes began early Monday, a ship caught fire in the strait of Hormuz near the coastline of Oman, the British military said. It was unclear what sparked the blaze off Oman, in the southern corridor the US military has encouraged ships to use to avoid Iran’s control. The Revolutionary Guards later claimed two oil tankers were blown up after attempting to transit through the southern route of the strait, but there was no independent confirmation.

The US and Iran have inched closer to all-out war as last month’s interim deal meant to permanently end the fighting has crumbled and shipping traffic in the strait of Hormuz has largely stalled.

Iran continued to retaliate by hitting US-allied countries throughout the Middle East, with sirens heard in Bahrain early on Monday. The US embassy in the country warned: “Iran may seek to target unspecified locations in central Manama”, the capital, without elaborating.

Iran’s Revolutionary Guards also claimed to have launched a “surprise attack” on an enemy command centre in the Al-Tanf region of Syria, in a statement reported by state news agency IRNA.

After Iran targeted sites in Jordan on Sunday, Israel warned of the danger of the strikes spilling across its borders, reigniting a direct regional conflict that had been restrained even as tensions between the Trump administration and Iran have soared again.

“If Iran fires missiles at Israel , we will attack them with full force,” said the Israeli defence minister, Israel Katz, on Sunday. The US is expected to send dozens of additional refuelling planes to Israel as part of a reinforcement of its force posture in the region, a US official told Reuters.

Trump has threatened to target Iran’s power stations and bridges to try to compel Tehran to loosen its hold on the strait of Hormuz, which saw one-fifth of global oil supplies transit before the war.

Last week the US reimposed a naval blockade on Iranian ports to halt its shipments of crude oil. The military on Saturday said it had redirected six ships and disabled one since then.
sources.headline: US launches ninth night of Iran strikes as strait of Hormuz standoff sees oil price pass $90 a barrel

sources.body:
Iranian media report blasts in cities in the far north and far south, including Tabriz and Chabahar.

Trump: Latest strikes on Iran 'in honour' of killed US military personnel

The United States has carried out its ninth consecutive night of air strikes against Iran, with Iranian media reporting explosions in multiple cities, including Sirik, Jask, Tabriz and Chabahar.

The attacks on Monday came as the US military confirmed the death of a third soldier in recent days, and as a month-old ceasefire agreement unravelled amid a fight for control of the Strait of Hormuz.

The US military said the attacks were aimed at “degrading Iranian military capabilities used to attack commercial vessels and mariners transiting the Strait of Hormuz”.

It said it targeted Iranian military command centres, air defence and coastal surveillance sites, maritime capabilities, missile and drone launch sites, as well as communications networks.

Explosions were reported across Iran, from the port cities of Bandar Mahshahr and Bandar Imam Khomeini in the Gulf to Jask and Sirik near the Strait of Hormuz, according to the state broadcaster IRIB. Air defence systems were also activated amid the sound of explosions in the southeastern cities of Konarak and Chabahar, according to the Fars news agency.

Further explosions were heard near the city of Khvormuj in Bushehr province and as far north as the city of Tabriz.

Al Jazeera’s Tohid Asadi, reporting from Tehran, said the latest US strikes again targeted Iran’s southern coast, an area that has been hit repeatedly over the past nine days, with earlier attacks damaging bridges, railway lines and an airport.

Jask and Sirik, both frequent targets, were struck again, while the explosions in Tabriz marked the first time the northwestern city had come under attack since the escalation began, he said.

While Iran has “witnessed yet another night of escalation”, Iranian leaders “are also talking about their retaliatory strikes”, he said, with state media saying Tehran had launched a wave of missiles from Lorestan province at US military bases and other assets across the region.

Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps (IRGC) said it retaliated with missile and drone strikes on Jordan and Kuwait. Meanwhile, sirens also sounded in Bahrain.

The IRGC said it had struck US aircraft at Jordan’s Aqaba airport and claimed to have destroyed hangars at the Muwaffaq Salti airbase in Azraq. It also claimed to have hit US military facilities at Ali Al Salem airbase in Kuwait.

In Washington, DC, US President Donald Trump told reporters the Pentagon was hitting Iran “very hard” to honour the three service members killed in Jordan and Iraq.

“We hit them very hard again tonight, and we did that in honour of the, probably three, it’s probably three great patriots,” Trump told reporters after returning to the US capital following the World Cup final. He said “we feel very badly” about the deaths, adding that “Iran cannot have a nuclear weapon”.

US Secretary of State Marco Rubio, meanwhile, told reporters in the Philippines that Washington was still open to diplomacy, but “it has to be real”. He urged “other countries” to “step up” to help protect shipping in the Strait of Hormuz, whether by providing “hardware or finances”.
sources.headline: US bombs Iran for ninth consecutive night as Hormuz tensions escalate

stories.headline: India cracks down on ‘Cockroach Party’ protests over exam scandal

sources.body:
Demonstrations are common across Indian states, but mass protests converging on the national capital's power centre have become relatively uncommon in recent years amid tighter policing, restrictions on public gatherings and a fragmented opposition.

Large mobilisations in central Delhi have historically accompanied moments of major political upheaval - from anti-corruption campaigns to farmers' protests - but such scenes have been rare around parliament itself.

That makes Monday's events significant, regardless of its immediate political impact: it is one of the biggest public displays of dissent in the capital against Prime Minister Narendra Modi's government in recent years.
sources.headline: Thousands of 'cockroach' protesters march towards India's parliament

sources.body: Police in New Delhi on Sunday banned a planned march on the Indian parliament by supporters of the viral, opposition Cockroach Janata Party. The demonstration set for Monday, the start of parliament's monsoonsession, follows growing calls for the education minister's resignation over exam paper leaks and the forced removal of a hunger-striking activist to hospital.
sources.headline: India bans march on parliament by ‘Cockroach’ activists, risking a standoff

stories.headline: Search underway after ferry with 133 people sinks off Guyana coast

sources.body:
Sixty-seven people have so far been rescued after a ferry carrying 116 passengers and 17 crew sunk off the coast of Guyana, local officials have said.

Fifteen children are among those recovered after the MV Barima capsized following its departure from the capital Georgetown on Saturday. Dozens of people are still missing.

A distress call was received at 23:01 local time (03:01 GMT Sunday) triggering a search operation involving government teams and privately-owned boats, Public Works Minister Juan Edghill said.

"We're hopeful we'll be able to account for all that were on board," he told reporters as emergency teams continued their search for survivors on Sunday afternoon.

Those rescued also include 41 men and 11 women, Prime Minister Mark Phillips told media. He added that the boat's captain was among that number.

The captain has since returned to the area to assist the "significantly increased" rescue operation, he added, as crews more than doubled their search area on Sunday.

Phillips said the boat had been within its weight capacity at the time of the incident, and that it was licensed to hold more than 300 passengers.

The names of those onboard will be released once rescue operations and medical checks are complete, he said on Facebook.

Assistance centres have been set up for relatives of those rescued and passengers still missing.

The MV Barima was travelling to Port Kaituma when it capsized near Iron Punt.

Edghill said it was fitted with 250 life jackets, two rigid life crafts and six inflatable life crafts.

It was built in 1939 and is 40m (131ft) long, according to a listing on VesselFinder.
sources.headline: Ferry carrying 133 passengers and crew sinks off Guyana coast

sources.body:
Authorities launch criminal investigation after passenger list – showing more than 100 passengers – was found to be inaccurate, complicating rescue efforts

A massive search-and-rescue operation is under way after a ferry sank ⁠off the coast of Guyana in South America ⁠late on Saturday, in what has been described by officials as one of the country’s worst maritime disasters.

Authorities launched a criminal investigation after a government official said the captain and a crew member of the ferry allegedly tested positive for marijuana and the vessel’s passenger manifest was found to be inaccurate.

Two people have been confirmed dead in the accident.

Authorities had previously said that there were 116 passengers and 17 crew members on board – as stated on the ship’s manifest – and confirmed that there was no mechanical problem and the vessel had been operating within its legal limits.

But speaking at a press conference alongside the country’s prime minister late on Sunday, Juan Edghill, Guyana’s minister of public works, told reporters the manifest was wrong after it was discovered that only 35 of 67 people rescued so far were on the passenger list.

The captain and the first engineer, both of whom allegedly tested positive for cannabis, are now in police custody.

“I am outraged. We are talking about operational breaches; people will be dealt with,” Edghill said at a press conference in Georgetown on Sunday night.

The ferry MV Barima left Georgetown, the capital of the English-speaking South American country, on Saturday at about 3.15pm for Port Kaituma, located in the oil-rich Essequibo region and just off the border with Venezuela.

A distress call was received shortly after 11pm, prompting an emergency response involving the Guyana Defence Force Coast Guard and Air Corps, private aircraft, fishing vessels and civilian volunteers.

Edghill had previously said the MV Barima was certified to carry 397 passengers and 284 tonnes of cargo. The ship was equipped with 250 lifejackets, two lifeboats and six inflatable rafts, Edghill had said.

The country’s prime minister Mark Phillips described the news that the manifest was incorrect as “deeply concerning and shocking”.

“The government has ordered an urgent and comprehensive investigation involving the Guyana Police Force, the Guyana Defence Force, the Maritime Administration Department, and other technical maritime experts,” Phillips said, adding that the accuracy of the passenger manifest and compliance with maritime safety regulations would be examined.

He added: “Let me be absolutely clear: where negligence, misconduct, or criminal wrongdoing is established, those responsible will face the full force of the law..”

Earlier on Sunday, Phillips said the search and rescue coordination centre had expanded the search area from 400 sq km (154 sq miles) to 1,070 sq km, with additional aircraft and vessels deployed alongside local fisherfolk familiar with the waters.

As the rescue effort continued, survivors recounted the moments when the ferry overturned in darkness.
sources.headline: Huge search operation after passenger ferry sinks off coast of Guyana

stories.headline: Magnitude-5.5 quake in Peru kills at least six and injures dozens

sources.body:
More than 30 injured and 300 displaced after buildings collapse near Sicaya

A 5.5-magnitude earthquake shook the Andes region of Peru , killing at least six people, authorities said Sunday. More than 30 people were injured and 300 have been displaced.

The US Geological Survey reported the quake struck on Saturday at 9.24pm, with its epicentre located 2km (1.2 miles) west-south-west of the city of Sicaya in Huancayo province. It was 10km (6.2 miles) deep.

Peru’s National Civil Defence Institute said in a statement that the total number of missing people is still unknown. Several buildings collapsed or suffered structural damage, including the local church and convent.

Luis Vásquez, head of the local civil defence office, told journalists that the use of rustic adobe materials in construction in that Andean area “has contributed to the greater impact and damage”.

Earthquakes are frequent in Peru, which is located in the Pacific “Ring of Fire”.

Images broadcast by local media captured the anguish of victims’ relatives in one of the hardest-hit areas, the agricultural region of Chongo Bajo, where residents huddled under blankets outside severely damaged homes. Animals were also seen under the rubble.

“My home has been destroyed,” Hermenegilda Guamalato told a local radio station as she sought a place to stay with her three children in the neighbouring district of Huayucachi.

In 2007, a 7.9-magnitude earthquake struck the province of Pisco in the Ica region, killing nearly 600 people.
sources.headline: Earthquake in Peru leaves at least six dead

stories.headline: Student loan defaults surge as pandemic-era protections lapse

sources.body:
Copyright 2026 The Associated Press. All Rights Reserved.

Copyright 2026 The Associated Press. All Rights Reserved.

Barbara Howaniec, a psychiatric nurse practitioner, poses for a photo in her office on Monday, June 15, 2026, in Portland, Maine. (AP Photo/Robert F. Bukaty)

Life after college did not go as Ashley Dreahn planned.

After starting out as a teacher, she went back to school — and took out more loans — in hopes of landing a higher-paying job in the chemical processing industry. It never materialized, and then came Hurricane Harvey, a job loss and a car breakdown. By 2022, she was so broke she filed for bankruptcy.

She found work at a Texas prison and was rebuilding her life, saving up for weight-loss surgery, when this spring she heard from a credit-monitoring service. The student loans she thought had been discharged in bankruptcy had ballooned to $94,298 with interest, and she had to start making payments. She was in default.

“I absolutely broke down,” said Dreahn, 40.

Across the country, the number of borrowers with defaulted student loans jumped by more than 4.2 million from April 2025 to March 2026, according to an Associated Press analysis . The surge includes many who went off track in 2024, when loan payments started coming due again after a pandemic-era freeze.

Hundreds of thousands more are months behind on payments, and another surge in defaults could be on the way. Millions of borrowers are facing higher monthly payments as the government dismantles its most affordable income-driven repayment option, the SAVE plan, one of several changes the Education Department says are intended to simplify a fragmented system.

Borrowers enter default after missing payments for nine months, and the consequences can upend lives. It hurts credit ratings and can lead to the debt being sent to collections.

“I am seeing despair and outrage and despondency and just a very wide mix of pretty extreme emotions, the likes of which I have not seen before,” said Alan Collinge, the founder of Student Loan Justice and the author of “The Student Loan Scam.”

The federal government can garnish wages and Social Security payments from borrowers in default, but the Trump administration in January walked back plans to begin collections on their loans. A Moody’s Analytics report this spring said garnishments are likely to begin within the next year, warning of “an additional headwind in an increasingly fragile economy.”

Dreahn enrolled at Texas Woman’s University in 2004 as the first in her family to go to college. She cobbled together jobs, babysitting and working at a call center and an automotive shop. But she still needed to borrow heavily.

“There was one point where they were like, ‘If you don’t come up with the money, we’re dropping you from your classes,’” said Dreahn, who earned a degree in history with an emphasis on education and social studies. “And it was, ‘OK, what do I need to sign?’”

She said no one explained what taking out loans truly meant.

“You just kind of trust these advisers and trust these financial aid people that this is what you’re supposed to do,” Dreahn said.
sources.headline: As defaults on student loans surge, millions are trying to get their lives back on track

sources.body:
Copyright 2026 The Associated Press. All Rights Reserved.

Copyright 2026 The Associated Press. All Rights Reserved.

The U.S. Department of Education building is photographed in Washington, Dec. 3, 2024. (AP Photo/Jose Luis Magana, File)

Defaults on student loans have surged across the United States, reaching record levels as borrowers struggle to keep up with payments.

The numbers have spiked since payments came due again following a lengthy pause intended to provide relief during the COVID-19 pandemic. Today, around 9.5 million people — 1 in 5 federal student loan borrowers — are in default, meaning they are more than nine months behind on their payments.

While credit scores can suffer when borrowers are just a few months behind, entering default brings the possibility of more serious consequences, including garnished wages or Social Security payments. For now, the Trump administration has held off on such involuntary collections.

“Folks are struggling to make ends meet and cover all the rising costs of everything else. The growing student loan bills are making things worse and folks are falling behind,” said Aissa Canchola Bañez, policy director for the advocacy group Protect Borrowers.

Here are some takeaways from an Associated Press analysis of student loan defaults.

The U.S. Education Department allowed borrowers to suspend federal student loan payments during the economic tumult of the pandemic. Though payments technically started coming due again in 2023, the Biden administration provided a one-year buffer period that ended in the fall of 2024.

Loans couldn’t enter default during this time, and federal programs designed to help delinquent borrowers and debt forgiveness initiatives brought millions out of default.

Starting in June 2025, with the pause having ended nine months prior, borrowers began defaulting again for the first time since the pandemic.

Since then, the number of defaulted borrowers has exploded from 5.3 million to around 9.5 million, according to data from the Office of Federal Student Aid. Out of $1.7 trillion in federally backed student loans nationwide, $233.3 billion is in default.

Another wave of defaults could be on the way. The Trump administration has eliminated the most generous income-driven repayment plan, Saving on a Valuable Education, or SAVE , as part of its overhaul of the federal student loan system. The millions of borrowers who had been enrolled in SAVE now will face the strain of paying more each month.

Starting this month, new borrowers pick between one standard repayment plan and one income-driven option, as opposed to having several options. The Education Department has described the changes as a simplification of a “fragmented and confusing” system.

Many of the states with the highest concentrations of defaulted borrowers are in the South, an AP analysis found.

Mississippi has the nation’s highest default rate at 28.3%, and others near the top include Louisiana, Alabama, West Virginia, Oklahoma, Georgia, South Carolina and Texas. Rounding out the 15 states with the highest default rates are Alaska, Arizona, Ohio, Indiana, Michigan, New Mexico and Nevada.
sources.headline: A wave of student loan borrowers have entered default since pandemic-era protections lapsed

stories.headline: Troy Jackson consolidates Democratic nod to take on Sen. Collins in Maine

sources.body: SANFORD, Maine — Troy Jackson has effectively secured the Democratic nomination in the crucial Maine Senate race, winning the support of a majority of the state delegates who will officially nominate a candidate next weekend. While they are not legally bound to support him, more than two-thirds of the 601 delegates have committed to voting for Jackson at that statewide convention. Jackson dominated the county-level meetings this weekend that selected those delegates, with his rivals falling far behind — and his two most organized competitors, Maine Secretary of State Shenna Bellows and former state public health official Nirav Shah, dropped out of the race on Sunday. The party’s statewide convention in Bangor, Maine, next weekend will cap a tumultuous scramble to replace Graham Platner, who won the primary but dropped out this month after POLITICO reported that a woman he previously dated said he forced her to have sex with him. Platner denied the allegations. A progressive 5th generation logger from the far-northern corner of Allagash, Maine, Jackson has had a long career in organizing progressive activists and ties to Sen. Bernie Sanders (I-Vt.), who had endorsed him in his gubernatorial race earlier this year. The candidate will have less than four months to spin up a major battleground Senate campaign against GOP Sen. Susan Collins, the only Senate Republican running for reelection in a state carried by former Vice President Kamala Harris in 2024. Jackson turned his attention to the general election at a Sunday morning rally ahead of the delegate meeting in York County, where he secured the support of enough delegates to cross the 50 percent threshold. “Whether you supported me from the beginning, or are still deciding, or support another candidate, there is a place for you in this movement,” he told a few dozen supporters in a school parking lot in Sanford, Maine, echoing what has been a familiar line in his speeches since entering the Senate race. “Because we're not going to defeat Susan Collins by making this movement smaller. There's no way that that's ever going to work.” Bellows dropped out of the race just minutes before voting began on Sunday and encouraged Democrats to coalesce behind Jackson. “This has been an unprecedented nominating process, compressed into days instead of months, and I'm grateful to every volunteer who worked around the clock for this movement,” she said in a statement . “That energy is exactly what we need to beat Susan Collins in November — and Democrats don't have a day to waste in unifying around that shared goal.” Jackson learned of his victory while speaking with Democrats at the Sagadahoc County meeting at a high school in Topsham, Maine, when an aide whispered in his ear the results of York County’s caucus. Jackson reacted with a small head nod before continuing his conversations with voters. News of his win ignited audible excitement from Jackson volunteers at the meeting. One organizer sprinted across the school’s gym to show Jackson the news on their phone. “It’s incredible,” Jackson told POLITICO moments after he secured a majority of delegates. “I’m excited, but very tempered, very measured about my emotions other than the fact that a lot of really great people made this happen.” Jackson’s whirlwind path to the nomination is the prologue to an even tougher test. Collins has defeated well-funded Democrats of all stripes since she was first elected in 1996, and currently has an $11 million war chest in reserves, according to the latest filings from the Federal Election Commission. And Jackson’s last-minute ascension puts him at an even greater disadvantage: Maine’s nominating convention on Saturday is just 101 days before Election Day in November, giving Jackson less time to run against Collins than Harris had in her losing campaign against Donald Trump in 2024. “We’re going to have to put our ground game even more into maximum overdrive,” Jackson told reporters after his Sanford rally, referencing Platner’s large number of town halls and his short timeline. “What did Graham have, 70 town halls? If we did one every other day we wouldn’t be able to do 70 from here on out.” Jackson also said he will have to quickly introduce himself to the southern part of the state, given he hails from the far-north and represented the area in the state Senate. “I’m not as well known down here,” he said. “I’m six hours away from my home. And as we know, the state legislature means nothing. Nobody’s following it. It’s a lot of work to make sure people know what I’m about.” Many former Platner supporters were part of the team of volunteers, organizers and delegate nominees that whipped votes for Jackson’s delegate slate in counties across Maine over the weekend. The strength of his organizing operation helped Jackson jump out to a commanding lead after Saturday’s meetings , including a sweep of all 149 delegates in Cumberland County, the largest county in the state. Travis Jones, a former Platner supporter from Seal Harbor, Maine, who was elected as a delegate alternate in Hancock County, where Platner lives, said he’s backing Jackson because of the similarities between Platner’s progressive agenda and the Jackson campaign. “I was a big supporter of the previous candidate, Graham Platner. I really liked his policies. Troy mirrors a lot of those,” Jones said ahead of the delegate meeting in Ellsworth, Maine, on Saturday. “The previous campaign really energized me, and here I am today.”
sources.headline: Troy Jackson has effectively secured the Maine Senate Democratic nomination

sources.body: SANFORD, Maine — Maine Secretary of State Shenna Bellows ended her Senate campaign on Sunday, further clearing the path for rival Troy Jackson as he consolidates support in a fast-moving Democratic nomination contest. "This has been an unprecedented nominating process, compressed into days instead of months, and I'm grateful to every volunteer who worked around the clock for this movement," Bellows said in a statement . "That energy is exactly what we need to beat Susan Collins in November — and Democrats don't have a day to waste in unifying around that shared goal." Bellows was the most prominent woman to enter the breakneck contest, which has unfolded over the last week after Graham Platner ended his campaign following POLITICO’s report that a woman who used to date him accused him of sexual assault. He has denied the allegations. Maine Democrats stood up a rapid nominating process, with county parties selecting delegates to go to a convention next weekend. It became quickly clear that Jackson, the former Maine Senate president who is aligned with organized labor and national progressives, was the frontrunner. On the first day of the local selection process, Jackson's volunteer operation dominated and won most of the slates. The stakes in the nomination fight are high for Democrats: Collins is among the most vulnerable Republican senators up for reelection this November. But she has been a resilient campaigner for decades in blue-leaning Maine, and the Platner fiasco may cast a shadow over the general election. Bellows was one of several Democrats, including Jackson, who ran for governor this year but lost the primary — and then jumped into the Senate contest after Platner dropped out. Her halting delivery during the Senate primary debate earlier this week and her struggle to stand out among the crowded field eventually led her to end her campaign.
sources.headline: Maine Democrat Shenna Bellows drops out of race to replace Graham Platner

stories.headline: States rush to outlaw speculative ticket resales after World Cup fiascos

sources.body: Stories of fans left outside stadiums, duped by “ghost tickets” on resale platforms, were everywhere at this year’s World Cup. But advocates are hopeful the near-endless stream of negative headlines and angry supporters will spur long-sought-after laws to outlaw the practice of speculative ticket selling, when resellers put tickets they don’t yet have on the open market. At the state level, it would seem they’re right. Illinois, North Carolina and Washington, D.C. have all passed legislation banning speculative ticket sales since the beginning of the World Cup. Connecticut, Oregon and Vermont have also enacted ghost ticket bans this year. And Massachusetts Gov. Maura Healey announced her intention to file a bill prohibiting ghost tickets — among other consumer-friendly initiatives — on Thursday. “It’s definitely raising eyebrows in the state of politicians and legislators to really ban ghost ticketing, speculative ticketing,” said Scott Friedman, podcast host and co-founder of the Ticket Talk Network. “When you have it happen at this scale, that not just affects the United States, it’s a global tournament for heaven’s sake. Absolutely, change is coming.” “You see dozens of these bills percolating around the country,” said John Breyault, vice president of public policy, telecommunications and fraud at the National Consumers League. “Most of which have some kind of ban on speculative tickets.” There’s action at the federal level, too. Though advocacy groups aren’t exactly aligned on a path forward in Congress. Breyault and the NCL have been active in promoting the TICKET Act, which passed the House in April and moved through the Senate Commerce Committee in June. Proponents say the bill would ban speculative ticket sales and safeguard consumers against fraudulent ticket websites. “In this environment, where Republicans and Democrats can’t agree that the sky is blue on some days, that’s a rare occurrence,” Breyault said. But Stephen Parker, the executive director of the National Independent Venue Association, said the “you could drive a Mack Truck” through the TICKET Act’s ghost ticketing provisions. Music groups, too, have panned the bill, arguing it would still allow speculative ticket sales by permitting sellers to advertise ticket procurement services for tickets they do not yet possess. For now, Parker said, the most encouraging action is happening in the states. “This is happening across the country, and ultimately, what we are seeing is state legislators from both parties saying enough is enough. If Congress isn’t going to do something, we are, and we couldn’t be more grateful for their work.”
sources.headline: Did World Cup permanently change ticket-price politics?

stories.headline: Los Angeles chases climate legacy from 2028 Olympics amid funding gaps

sources.body: Los Angeles leaders are spending billions to make the 2028 Olympics a story of climate and environmental policy progress, reports Alex Nieves. Transit and community groups want to know whether the investment will be part of the region’s Olympic legacy or just a flash in the pan. The question of just how enduring their efforts will be was a throughline Tuesday and Wednesday at the Los Angeles Cleantech Incubator’s Road to 2028 summit — an event focused on leveraging the Olympic Games to accelerate clean energy and transportation development in the state’s most populous region. The overall vibe of the conference was an optimistic one, as Los Angeles County and dozens of cities are hustling to secure hundreds of buses, plant trees and clean waterways before millions of people descend on the region in two years for the Games. But some attendees were already wondering what comes after the two-week mega event is over. “When we look to the future, if once the games leave and we have not left communities better than the way we found them, then we have all lost,” said Cynthia Mitchell-Heard, CEO of the Los Angeles Urban League, speaking on a panel of grant recipients who’ve received funding from the LA Olympics organizing committee for environmental projects. (The Urban League received $100,000 to create a workforce development program for people affected by last year’s Altadena fire.) Catherine Baltazar, associate director of advocacy and organizing at Climate Resolve, argued that throwing dollars at infrastructure projects won’t guarantee long-term progress in communities that have historically lacked resources and disproportionately borne the brunt of climate change. “Investment should not end when the Games end,” Baltazar said. “We know that these communities will continue long after the Games, and so for me, that’s the legacy that I want.” The focus on the post-2028 future comes in part because of what was seen as a successful test run during this summer’s World Cup. The region hosted eight matches at SoFi Stadium in Inglewood, avoiding any major snafus while seeing a significant spike in public transit use. While the Olympics will be far larger than the World Cup, the region has structural advantages over many past Olympic host cities, including enough existing venues to house every sporting event. Much of the preparation now is centered on buying and leasing roughly 1,700 buses — including 500 electric school buses — to shuttle fans to and from venues that will be transit-only. Transit advocates argue that those investments, including recent expansions of Los Angeles’ light-rail system, don’t solve for structural budget deficits that could threaten service cuts — as bus and rail operators have already warned about farther north in the Bay Area . Los Angeles’ legislative delegation has failed the last two years to secure a boost in the state budget for systems like LA Metro, which faces a $2.3 billion deficit through 2030. Metrolink, a commuter rail line that serves six counties across Southern California, has indefinitely extended a 20 percent reduction in weekday service amid a $30 million deficit. Matt Petersen, CEO of the Cleantech Incubator, told POLITICO the budget challenge has only intensified under a Trump administration hostile toward clean transportation. “Local governments are struggling, states are struggling on how to put the resources where they’re needed,” he said. “I think the bottom line is: Without the feds, it’s super hard.” But he said he still believes that Los Angeles is “well on the way to being ready” for the Games and to leave a lasting benefit for the community. “That’s really what we’re here to do,” he said. “Ten years after the Games, what will we have seen manifest because of this moment?”
sources.headline: LA’s next mega-event test: The coming climate Olympics

stories.headline: Push for FIFA transgender ban meets resistance from anti-discrimination partner

sources.body: The head of FIFA’s anti-discrimination partner is calling on the international soccer body to not adopt a ban on transgender athletes at the Women’s World Cup, after the Trump administration publicly announced its intention to pressure the organization to do so last week. Andrew Giuliani, the head of the White House World Cup Task Force, told POLITICO Thursday that getting FIFA to guarantee that “women play in the Women’s World Cup and not biological men” could represent a lynchpin issue during planning negotiations for the 2031 tournament. If successful, the endeavor would represent a major victory for the Trump administration’s efforts to bar transgender women from competing in women’s and girls’ sports domestically and abroad. Earlier this year, the International Olympic Committee adopted a blanket ban for its female categories that paralleled an earlier White House executive order . But Piara Powar, the executive director of anti-discrimination group Fare network, warned that such a restriction would only further stigmatize transgender athletes and marginalize women with intersex characteristics. “We think it’s important that even if a ban comes into force for the World Cup in 2031 it should not be embedded in FIFA regulations permanently,” Powar wrote in a text message to POLITICO. “FIFA should not go the way of the IOC and exclude human beings from participation in football.” FIFA did not respond to requests for comment. The organization currently has no centralized sex-verification regime, instead requiring each national federation to verify the eligibility of its players before submitting rosters, including investigating “any perceived deviation in secondary sex characteristics” and maintaining documentation of its findings. The IOC’s policy, meanwhile, dictates that athletes competing in female categories undergo a one-time genetic screening. Powar said such a ban at FIFA would impact women with medical conditions known as differences in sex development, or DSDs, which can manifest as testosterone levels outside the typical female range and having male chromosomes. Jon Holmes, spokesperson for the advocacy group Football v Homophobia similarly warned that women with DSDs would “bear the brunt of this.” He suggested that the administration’s efforts are largely performative — noting that “there’s no suggestion anyone is near international level,” referring to transgender female soccer players. Payoshni Mitra, an advocate for abolishing sex testing in women’s sports, however, said in an interview Friday that it was too early to speculate about a policy that doesn’t exist, but noted that she “will be very, very surprised if suddenly they come up with a very exclusionary policy.” Mitra said she’d hope that FIFA acts “in a way that is based on science,” adding: "If they come up with a policy, the policy will be based on science, evidence-based science, and also should take into account athlete well-being and the other conditions that concern these policies.” FIFA has yet to formally announce the location of the 2031 tournament, for which the U.S., Mexico, Costa Rica and Jamaica have submitted the sole joint bid. Giuliani said Thursday that the organization had already told President Donald Trump that the U.S. would host the contest. Powar pleaded with other sporting groups to consider the human price of hosting their tournaments in the U.S., despite the lucrative commercial appeal of the country. “We would urge all sports bodies to think of the credibility of their sport and the harmful impact of accepting all U.S. conditions of hosting — it may come at the cost of excluding participants,” Powar wrote. “And in football, a sport that has always been the most open and accessible, that could be devastating.”
sources.headline: FIFA anti-discrimination partner warns against Olympic-style transgender ban

stories.headline: Sánchez heads to Algeria to repair ties and secure more gas supplies

sources.body: <p>El presidente del Gobierno, Pedro Sánchez, visitará este lunes Argel para pactar un aumento del suministro de gas y culminar la superación de la crisis diplomática bilateral que se abrió en marzo de 2022, después de que <a href="https://elpais.com/espana/2022-03-18/sanchez-considera-la-autonomia-del-sahara-como-la-base-mas-seria-realista-y-creible-para-resolver-el-conflicto.html" target="_self" rel="" title="https://elpais.com/espana/2022-03-18/sanchez-considera-la-autonomia-del-sahara-como-la-base-mas-seria-realista-y-creible-para-resolver-el-conflicto.html">España rompiera en favor de Marruecos </a>su tradicional neutralidad en el conflicto del Sáhara. Sánchez viajará directamente desde Nueva York, ciudad a la que se había desplazado previamente para asistir a la final de la Copa del Mundo de fútbol, y se entrevistará con el primer ministro argelino, Sifi Ghrieb, y el presidente, Abdelmayid Tebún, además de mantener un encuentro con empresarios españoles que operan en el país.</p><p><a href="https://elpais.com/espana/2026-07-20/sanchez-culmina-en-argel-la-superacion-de-la-crisis-que-provoco-el-giro-sobre-el-sahara.html" target="_blank">Seguir leyendo</a></p>
sources.headline: Sánchez culmina en Argel la superación de la crisis que provocó el giro sobre el Sáhara

sources.body:
El presidente del Gobierno, Pedro Sánchez , aterriza este lunes en Argelia , procedente desde Nueva York, donde asistió en la víspera al final del Mundial de Fútbol que dio la Copa a España. Sánchez busca en este país africano cerrar la crisis por el giro de su Ejecutivo sobre el Sáhara y garantizarse el suministro de gas ante el bloqueo del estrecho de Ormuz .

Sánchez solo ha viajado a Argelia una vez desde que es presidente. Fue en 2020, dos años antes del giro histórico en la posición de España sobre el Sáhara, cuando decidió respaldar el plan de autonomía de Marruecos . Un apoyo que deterioró la relación con el país magrebí, que llegó incluso a retirar a su embajador en Madrid y a suspender el Tratado de Amistad, Buena Vecindad y Cooperación .

Fue a partir de 2023 cuando comenzó un deshielo gradual que supuso el regreso del embajador, la recuperación de la interlocución diplomática y el restablecimiento de las relaciones comerciales, hasta que en la visita a Argel que hizo el pasado marzo el ministro de Asuntos Exteriores, José Manuel Albares, el presidente argelino, Abdelmayid Tebboune, le comunicó la reactivación del Tratado de Amistad.

Tebboune se reunirá con Sánchez , una imagen con la que fuentes del Gobierno subrayan que se escenificará la plena recuperación de la normalidad en las relaciones entre los dos países en un proceso en el que consideran que ha influido positivamente la posición de España ante la crisis en Oriente Medio y, en concreto, a favor del pueblo palestino ante la situación en Gaza.

Sánchez se desplaza a Argel desde Nueva York tras presenciar la final del Mundial de fútbol en la que España se impuso a Argentina y se hizo con su segunda Copa, y estará acompañado en esta visita por Albares y por su vicepresidenta tercera y ministra de Transición Ecológica, Sara Aagesen.

La presencia de Aagesen evidencia la relevancia que otorga el Gobierno a uno de los objetivos de este viaje, el relacionado con el ámbito energético.

Prueba de ello es que este lunes estarán también en la capital argelina los representantes de las cuatro empresas españolas clave en este sector: Repsol, Naturgy, Moeve y Enagás.

Argelia es el principal suministrador de gas a España (que es el segundo comprador europeo a ese país solo por detrás de Italia), y el Gobierno considera que aún hay potencial para aumentar esas importaciones y asegura que también hay interés para ello por parte de las autoridades argelinas, que aspiran igualmente a recibir más inversiones en este sector.

Según el último boletín estadístico publicado por Enagás este mes, el gas natural procedente de Argelia representa casi un 34% del total recibido por España en el primer semestre, por delante de Estados Unidos, con un 29,4%.

La mayor parte llega a España a través del gasoducto Medgaz, que conecta por el Mediterráneo los yacimientos argelinos con Almería, aunque hay otra (algo más de un 10%) que llega como gas natural licuado (GNL) en barcos metaneros.

El objetivo es aumentar la importación de gas por las dos vías , aunque el Gobierno resalta la estabilidad especial que aporta el hacerlo mediante tubo y baraja la posibilidad de que ese incremento pueda situarse en torno a un 10%.
sources.headline: S�nchez busca en Argelia cerrar la crisis por el S�hara y garantizar el suministro de gas ante el bloqueo de Ormuz

stories.headline: Spanish government prepares housing decree and health measures to regain momentum

sources.body: <p>El Gobierno quiere cerrar con un buen sabor de boca un curso traumático por la sucesión de <a href="https://elpais.com/espana/2026-06-22/el-supremo-condena-a-abalos-a-24-anos-y-tres-meses-de-carcel-a-koldo-garcia-a-19-anos-y-ocho-meses-y-a-aldama-a-cuatro-anos-y-medio.html" target="_self" rel="" title="https://elpais.com/espana/2026-06-22/el-supremo-condena-a-abalos-a-24-anos-y-tres-meses-de-carcel-a-koldo-garcia-a-19-anos-y-ocho-meses-y-a-aldama-a-cuatro-anos-y-medio.html">reveses judiciales.</a> Pedro Sánchez aspira a aprobar antes del parón del verano un decreto de vivienda, negociado con los socios del bloque de la investidura. El Ejecutivo, que tiene previsto aprobar leyes de sanidad sobre medicamentos y tabaco en el Consejo de Ministros de este martes, no contempla un Consejo extraordinario solo para vivienda, por lo que el calendario solo dejaría de margen el Consejo de Ministros del 28 de julio. Ese día será, además, cuando el presidente haga su tradicional balance de fin del curso político.</p><p><a href="https://elpais.com/espana/2026-07-20/el-gobierno-trata-de-recuperar-la-iniciativa-con-un-decreto-sobre-vivienda-y-medidas-en-sanidad.html" target="_blank">Seguir leyendo</a></p>
sources.headline: El Gobierno trata de retomar la iniciativa con un ‘sprint’ final con políticas de vivienda y sanidad

stories.headline: Wildfire in Guadalajara burns 16,000 hectares and forces fresh evacuations

sources.body:
España afronta este lunes una jornada de extrema gravedad debido a los incendios forestales que afectan especialmente a las provincias de Guadalajara y Zaragoza , presentando dos escenarios marcadamente distintos.

Mientras que en la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara el fuego se mantiene fuera de toda capacidad de extinción tras haber devorado ya 16.000 hectáreas en un perímetro de 100 kilómetros , la situación en Zaragoza ofrece un leve respiro con la estabilización del incendio de Orés , permitiendo que se empiece a planificar el regreso de los ciudadanos a sus hogares bajo estrictas medidas de seguridad.

En la provincia de Guadalajara, la situación ha sido calificada como de "riesgo extremo" por el director técnico de extinción, Gabriel García Marco. Las llamas, que se originaron el pasado jueves en el municipio de La Mierla , han mostrado un comportamiento extremadamente agresivo.

Durante las últimas horas, la velocidad de propagación del fuego se ha duplicado, pasando de los 2 kilómetros por hora iniciales a los 4 kilómetros por hora registrados este domingo, impulsados por rachas de viento que han alcanzado los 50 kilómetros por hora .

Esta evolución desfavorable ha obligado a las autoridades a tomar medidas drásticas para proteger a la población civil. Según los datos de la Consejería de Desarrollo Sostenible, actualmente hay 20 poblaciones evacuadas , lo que supone un total de 800 personas desplazadas de sus domicilios. La Guardia Civil acaba de evacuar otras seis localidades a primera hora de este lunes. Son las de Riofrío del Llano, La Bodera, Cardeñosa, Rebollosa de Jadraque, Angón y Santiuste .

Además, durante la tarde del domingo se decretó el confinamiento de otros cuatro pueblos: Ujados, La Miñosa, Albendiego y Cañamares.

La prioridad absoluta del dispositivo de emergencia es, en estos momentos, la defensa de los núcleos de población situados dentro del perímetro del incendio, ya que la intensidad de las llamas impide realizar un ataque directo sobre la cabeza del fuego.

El avance del incendio en Guadalajara se dirige actualmente hacia las faldas del pico de Alto Rey . Las previsiones técnicas apuntan a que el fuego rebasará la cresta de esta elevación en las próximas horas debido a las condiciones meteorológicas adversas, caracterizadas por altas temperaturas, baja humedad relativa y vientos fuertes .

A pesar de la gravedad general, existen puntos de esperanza : en la vertiente oeste del río Sorbe , una zona con gran potencial de propagación, los equipos de extinción lograron contener las llamas desde los primeros días, evitando reactivaciones en ese flanco.

El operativo desplegado cuenta con la participación de 320 efectivos terrestres y 26 medios aéreos que trabajan de forma ininterrumpida.

Por otro lado, en la provincia de Zaragoza, el consejero de Interior del Gobierno de Aragón, Roberto Bermúdez de Castro, ha informado de que el incendio de Orés se encuentra estabilizado . Aunque la estabilización no implica que el fuego esté controlado ni extinguido , permite empezar a trabajar en un escenario de vuelta a la normalidad que podría iniciarse este lunes.
sources.headline: Incendios en Espa�a: gravedad extrema en Guadalajara con 16.000 hect�reas quemadas y leve respiro en Zaragoza

sources.body:
El incendio que se inició el pasado jueves en La Mierla, en la Sierra Norte de Guadalajara  

El incendio de La Mierla (Guadalajara) declarado el pasado jueves afecta este domingo por la tarde a 16.000 hectáreas en un perímetro de 100 kilómetros, deja 20 pueblos evacuados, cinco confinados y unas 800 personas desplazadas. Los nuevos municipios confinados son Ujados, La Miñosa, Albendiego y Cañamares.

Así lo han trasladado en atención a los medios Daniel García, director técnico de extinción y el viceconsejero de Medio Ambiente, José Almodóvar.

La titular de la Consejería de Desarrollo Sostenible, Mercedes Gómez, explicó este domingo que la evacuación de las poblaciones tiene como finalidad facilitar el establecimiento de líneas de defensa y de contención de las llamas, que durante la pasada noche avanzaron 14 kilómetros, de forma que la superficie afectada asciende a 16.000 hectáreas.

Gómez explicó que se está procediendo a hacer cortafuegos de contención intentando dirigir el fuego, “y no que el fuego sea el que nos dirija a nosotros”, precisó, y establecer líneas de densa que posibiliten la extinción, y para ello ha sido necesario evacuar a más poblaciones.

Además, se está procediendo a la suspensión de determinadas actividades que se desarrollan en el entorno de la zona incendiada o en las cercanías, así como en áreas más alejadas del incendio pero que están siendo afectadas por el humo y la ceniza, como es el caso de varios campamentos juveniles.

En este sentido, Gómez indicó que se están dando instrucciones para que se suspendan estos campamentos, así como que se pare cautelarmente la actividad de una mina de sílice que hay en Naharros.

En cuanto al dispositivo de extinción, Gómez informó que este sábado estuvieron trabajando sobre el terreno unos 500 efectivos del plan de incendios de Castilla-La Mancha (Infocam), de la UME, del Ministerio para la Transición Ecológica (Miteco), de la Comunidad de Madrid y de la Diputación de Guadalajara.

Entre los medios que estaban en el dispositivo había 25 medios aéreos que estaban operando de forma simultánea, lo que es muy difícil de gestionar, apuntó Gómez, que comentó también que se están incremento el 30% de las plantillas, debido a la dimensión que ha cobrado el incendio.

Sus dimensiones hacen necesario consolidar incluso la zona ya quemada, porque todavía puede haber posibles reproducciones, por lo que se necesitan más contingentes para poder actuar en la defensa de las zonas por las que ya han pasado las llamas, así como la defensa de las casas de todas las localidades desalojadas, comentó la consejera de Desarrollo Sostenible.

Una vez pasada la noche los medios aéreos retomaron su actividad, y en estos momentos hay un total de 204 personas trabajando a lo largo de 32 equipos, según la información dispuesta por el Infocam.

Por su parte, el Gobierno de Castilla-La Mancha se encuentra perfilando una estrategia de actuación en la zona afectada por el incendio de La Mierla de cara a impulsar tanto al sector primario como al turístico cuando regrese la normalidad.
sources.headline: El fuego de Guadalajara quema 16.000 hectáreas

stories.headline: Raúl Moreno becomes Catalonia’s new social rights minister after cabinet reshuffle

sources.body: (ACN) Raúl Moreno serà el conseller de Drets Socials i Inclusió en substitució de Mònica Martínez Bravo. Ho ha avançat El Periódico i ho ha fet públic el president de la Generalitat, Salvador Illa, en una declaració institucional. Moreno ocupava fins ara la secretaria general del departament. Per tant, es tracta d’un relleu de continuïtat . És educador social i ha estat gairebé 10 anys diputat del PSC al Parlament. Anteriorment, va exercir de regidor de Santa Coloma de Gramenet durant 12 anys. El president de la Generalitat, Salvador Illa, ha anunciat el primer canvi del seu govern , que coincideix amb l’equador de la legislatura. I ha destacat que, per primera vegada, encapçalarà el departament un conseller que ha estat educador social. La refundació de la DGAIA L’agost del 2024, Martínez Bravo va deixar la secretaria general d’Inclusió al Ministeri d’Inclusió, Seguretat Social i Migracions per entrar a formar part de l’executiu d’Illa. En aquests últims dos anys, ha afrontat la refundació de l’antiga DGAIA , que s’ha convertit en la Direcció General de Prevenció i Protecció a la Infància i l’Adolescència (DGPPIA). Un òrgan assenyalat per la Sindicatura de Comptes per pagaments indeguts i que ha estat qüestionat en els darrers anys. Especialment, després del cas de la menor víctima d’una trama de prostitució que va patir abusos sexuals mentre estava sota tutela de la Generalitat . Per motius familiars, Martínez Bravo ha viscut els últims dos anys a cavall entre Madrid i Barcelona.
sources.headline: Raúl Moreno serà el conseller de Drets Socials en substitució de Martínez Bravo

sources.body:
El president de la Generalitat, Salvador Illa, con la hasta ahora consellera de Drets Socials, Mònica Martínez Bravo, y Raúl Moreno, que será su sucesor / Raquel Pérez

El president de la Generalitat, Salvador Illa , se dispone a ejecutar el primer cambio en su Govern de la legislatura. EL PERIÓDICO ha avanzado en exclusiva que la consellera de Drets Socials i Inclusió, Mònica Martínez Bravo , no continuará al frente del departamento a petición propia desde este mismo martes y será relevada en las próximas horas. Con su salida, el hasta ahora secretario general de la conselleria, Raúl Moreno , graduado en Educación Social, asumirá esta cartera con vocación de dar continuidad a la agenda desplegada hasta ahora en este ámbito. La previsión es que sea el único cambio que se produzca en el Consell Executiu justo a las puertas del parón estival y de que se cumplan dos años de la investidura de Illa.

En una comparecencia institucional, el president ha confirmado el cambio anunciado por este diario y concretado que Moreno será oficialmente nombrado en las próximas horas para que este martes participe ya de la reunión del Consell Executiu. A Martínez Bravo ha agradecido su "dedicación y compromiso" por haber afrontado el rediseño del sistema de protección social y de Moreno ha destacado su larga trayectoria "a favor de la justicia social".

La consellera ha tomado la decisión de irse por motivos personales y así se lo comunicó hace unas semanas al president, que ha mantenido una absoluta discreción hasta el punto de que no ha sido hasta el último momento cuando el conjunto de consellers ha conocido el cambio. En marzo del año pasado, este diario ya explicó que, por circunstancias familiares, la dirigente vivía a caballo entre Barcelona y Madrid , por lo que concentraba su agenda pública de lunes a jueves para conciliar, una dinámica que se ha mantenido y que ha sido objeto de crítica por parte de los partidos de la oposición por considerar que se trata de una situación que no es habitual en un miembro del Govern.

Martínez Bravo fue una de las independientes por las que Illa apostó para incorporar al Govern tras su investidura el 8 de agosto de 2024. Doctora en Economía por el Massachusetts Institute of Technology y licenciada en Economía por la Universidad Pompeu Fabra, dejó la secretaria general de Inclusión en el Ministerio de Inclusión, Seguridad Social y Migraciones para dar el salto a la Generalitat. Con un perfil eminentemente técnico, la mayor crisis que ha tenido que gestionar durante estos casi dos años ha sido la de las presuntas irregularidades en la DGAIA y las polémicas sobre el sistema de protección a la infancia , un brete heredado de los gobiernos anteriores que acabó con la disolución de este organismo y la creación de la Direcció General de Prevenció i Protecció a la Infància i l'Adolescència (DGPPIA).

El president de la Generalitat, Salvador Illa, junto a la consellera Mònica Martínez Bravo en el Palau / Enric Fontcuberta / EFE

La consellera también ha priorizado desde el inicio de la legislatura la reducción de las listas de espera en dependencia y la modernización del sistema de prestaciones sociales. Recientemente ha puesto también en marcha el 'pla Cura' para lograr que la ayuda por dependencia se acabe recibiendo en un plazo de 60 días y que los casos más graves cobren una prestación automática de 200 euros, una apuesta que el mismo president Illa ha definido como la "transformación más ambiciosa" desde que existe la ley.
sources.headline: Raúl Moreno, nuevo conseller de Drets Socials en sustitución de Mònica Martínez Bravo

stories.headline: L9 metro works spark sinkholes and fresh safety fears in Sant Gervasi

sources.body:
Els veïns de Sant Gervasi – la Bonanova s’han pres l’ esvoranc detectat al carrer de Teodora Lamadrid com un avís a navegants dels estralls que pot provocar el pas de la tuneladora de l’L9 per sota de les seves cases. Diversos residents al barri s’han acostat aquest dimecres al migdia a la reunió informativa convocada per informar els inquilins de les finques desallotjades preventivament pel moviment de terres amb l’objectiu de conèixer de primera mà les causes i saber si es prendran mesures per evitar que es aquests es puguin tornar a repetir. Entre els assistents a la trobada hi havia en Lluís Roger Berenguer , membre de l’ Associació de Veïns de Sant Gervasi de Cassoles i una de les persones que viu en un edifici ubicat en el recorregut per on ha de passar la màquina que excava el túnel.

“ Som de la zona que pot resultar afectada imminentment. Ens han dit que s’ha fet una revisió del procediment del treball, però necessitem més informació perquè la tuneladora segueix treballant “, assegura. Tot i celebrar que tothom que ho ha requerit hagi pogut ser atès pels serveis municipals, Berenguer indica que ara el gran problema serà veure si les asseguradores de cadascuna de les finques i les llars es fan càrrec de tot plegat. També reconeix que tant ell com els seus veïns estan en alerta davant possibles noves afectacions que es detectin pel pas de la màquina. “ No hi ha una alarma elevada, però sí preocupació. Son moltes cases i no hi ha garanties “, afirma.

Cal recordar que fins ara hi ha vuit blocs desallotjats i que els seus veïns i negocis no podran tornar-hi almenys fins d’aquí a tres dies . Durant el matí del dissabte es valorarà si es pot efectuar un retorn progressiu a les finques afectades, tal com ha explicat aquest migdia el director general d’Infraestructures de la Generalitat, Ramon Ramírez.
sources.headline: Sant Gervasi, en alerta davant noves afectacions per l’L9: “No hi ha garanties”

sources.body:
Veïns del barri de Sant Gervasi van avisar fa dies de l'aparició d'escletxes a casa seva, coincidint amb els treball de la tuneladora que treballa a la línia 9 del metro .

És el cas de les religioses mercedàries missioneres del convent situat al carrer Sant Gervasi de Cassoles, que van detectar moviments i esquerdes a finals de maig. Els resultats són ben visibles a la planta baixa de l'edifici, on hi ha algunes rajoles trencades i moltes d'aixecades, i es veuen esquerdes a diverses parets.

Tan bon punt van detectar-ho van avisar les autoritats, i des d'aleshores tècnics d'Infraestructures de la Generalitat han estat instal·lant-hi aparells i fent-hi controls.

" Cada matí venen un grup de topògrafs que controlen el moviment de l'edifici. Hi tenim diversos sensors i també una mena de disc dur que registra els moviments", explica la membre de les mercedàries Maria Carlina a 3CatInfo.

La religiosa explica que van descobrir la destrossa "la nit del 27 al 28 de maig, quan va passar la tuneladora just per sota del pati ". "Vam sentir molts moviments i és quan es van fer les primeres fissures", recorda.

De fet, també en aquell moment es va fer una esquerda al carrer , davant del convent. "La vorera es va arreglar provisionalment perquè la gent no s'hi entrebanqués", assenyala.

Després de l'esvoranc que s'ha obert aquest dimarts ben a prop d'allà , uns tècnics s'han tornat a desplaçar a la zona per instal·lar-hi nous sensors per mesurar l'evolució dels moviments.

Malgrat la col·locació d'aquests nous punts de control, els veïns recorden que des de principis d'any tenen sensors a les façanes dels habitatges.

Josep Maria Olivé, un dels desallotjats, lamenta que "amb sensors o sense, l'esvoranc s'hauria fet igualment", i es pregunta "de què han servit" .

Els tècnics de l'obra han explicat a 3CatInfo que aquests pilots han permès saber que hi havia hagut moviments verticals a la zona abans del primer avís de l'aparició de la cavitat.
sources.headline: El convent on s'ha aixecat el terra coincidint amb les obres de l'L9: "Cada dia venen topògrafs"

stories.headline: Barcelona readies extreme-heat drill as tropical nights set new records

sources.body:
L'Ajuntament de Barcelona ha oficialitzat aquest dimecres el projecte " Barcelona a 50 °C: assajar avui per respondre millor demà" per experimentar com funciona la ciutat en escenaris de calor extrema i reforçar la seva estratègia per minimitzar els efectes del canvi climàtic.

La iniciativa, presentada al Saló de Cent del consistori barcelonès, està inclosa dins el Pla Calor 2025-2035 i vol contribuir a preparar la ciutat per afrontar les temperatures cada cop més altes que es poden arribar a registrar en les pròximes dècades.

En declaracions aquest dijous al programa "Matins", Irma Ventayol , directora de serveis de l' Oficina de Canvi Climàtic i Sostenibilitat de l'Ajuntament de Barcelona, ha explicat que el consistori té clar què ha de fer per fer front a la calor actual, però necessiten acabar d'imaginar-se què caldria fer per afrontar un futur on s'arribi a 50 graus:

Hem de descobrir quines són aquelles coses que, en les properes dècades, hem de començar a treballar.

Ventayol ha recordat els efectes que les últimes onades de calor han tingut a diferents països europeus , amb casos on l'asfalt s'ha desfet, les vies s'han dil·latatat i el transport públic ha deixat de funcionar, o els serveis sanitaris han col·lapsat:

Volem començar a treballar tot això ara, perquè si hem de començar a substituir materials, tinguem entre 20 i 30 anys per fer-ho.

Ventayol ha explicat que, en cas que Barcelona arribi a 50 graus, ens podríem trobar amb una situació similar a la que es va viure durant l'apagada . En aquest cas, la causa seria un ús massiu de l'aire condicionat que portaria la xarxa a col·lapsar:

Ens podríem trobar amb diferents efectes en cascada.

"Et falla l' electricitat , et deixen de funcionar les telecomunicacions o pots arribar a tenir problemes d' abastament aigua als barris de muntanya, per exemple", explica la directora de serveis de l'Oficina de Canvi Climàtic, reflexionant sobre què podria passar.

L'objectiu del projecte és descobrir què podria passar i elaborar els protocols necessaris per evitar-ho.
sources.headline: Barcelona oficialitza el projecte per preparar un simulacre d'un episodi de calor extrema

sources.body: El dia acaba, però la calor no sempre marxa amb el capvespre. La temperatura elevada, qui més qui menys, la pot acabar esquivant durant el dia, però quan es fa fosc trobar-hi refugi és molt més difícil. Dit d’una altra manera, la diferència entre suportar la calor de dia i de nit és que la primera, sovint, té escapatòria; la segona, no sempre. Les dades de l’estació oficial del Meteocat al Raval ho reflecteixen bé: fins al 18 de juliol ja s’hi han registrat 48 nits tropicals, una xifra elevada i en la línia dels estius més càlids de les darreres dècades. El que sí que marca un abans i un després és el nombre de nits tòrrides, que enguany ja és el més alt de tota la sèrie. També et pot interessar Barcelona registra el nombre més alt de nits tropicals dels darrers anys Ariadna Corominas del Hoyo 2 minuts de lectura El termòmetre nocturn escriu un nou rècord al Raval Per analitzar l’evolució de la calor nocturna al Raval, s’han revisat les dades de l’estació oficial del Meteocat des del 2009 fins al 2026 i s’ha fixat sempre el mateix període de comparació: de l’1 de gener al 18 de juliol , ambdós inclosos. En aquest interval, el 2026 acumula 48 nits tropicals, una de les xifres més elevades de tota la sèrie, només per darrere de les 52 del 2022 i igual que les 48 del 2025. La dada que més canvia la fotografia és la de les nits tòrrides, aquelles en què la temperatura mínima no baixa dels 25 ºC. Fins al 18 de juliol, el Raval n’ha registrat 23, el valor més alt de tota la sèrie . És una situació que contrasta amb molts dels primers anys analitzats: entre el 2009 i el 2021 hi va haver diversos períodes en què, fins aquesta data, no s’hi havia registrat cap nit tòrrida o només se n’havien comptabilitzat de manera puntual . El canvi més evident és que, en els últims anys, aquestes nits han deixat de ser una excepció dins del recompte de nits tropicals. També et pot interessar La calor va per barris: els més vulnerables s’escalfen 1°C més que la resta Ariadna Corominas del Hoyo 2 minuts de lectura La diferència entre una nit tropical i una nit tòrrida és només de cinc graus en la temperatura mínima —el llindar passa dels 20 ºC als 25 ºC—, però climàticament descriu dues situacions diferents: una nit en què la temperatura baixa poc i una altra en què pràcticament no hi ha alleujament tèrmic durant la matinada . En resum, les dades no indiquen que el 2026 tingui més nits tropicals que mai, però sí que mostren que aquestes nits càlides arriben amb una intensitat més gran que en molts dels anys anteriors de la sèrie. nou canal de WhatsApp Vols rebre cada dia la previsió del temps per WhatsApp? Segueix el canal de betevé el temps 🔔 I no t’oblidis d’activar la campaneta!
sources.headline: La calor nocturna s’intensifica al Raval: més nits tropicals acaben sent tòrrides
